rationale: This is the classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction, using tert-butanol as the carbocation precursor and acetonitrile as the nitrile source. The reaction is promoted by a strong acid (sulfuric acid) to generate the tert-butyl cation, which is then trapped by the nitrile. Subsequent hydrolysis of the nitrilium ion intermediate yields N-tert-butylacetamide. This example clearly illustrates the core transformation: the formation of a C-N bond between a tertiary carbocation and a nitrile, followed by hydrolysis to an N-substituted amide.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic, saturated aliphatic substrates (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with other named reactions. It avoids arenes (Friedel-Crafts), alkenes (Markovnikov additions, hydroboration), or other functional groups that could trigger different named reaction classifications. The core transformation is clearly the acid-catalyzed addition of a nitrile to a carbocation to form an amide, which is the defining feature of the Ritter reaction.
